element.value: Avoid using eval() — it allows execution of arbitrary PHP code and leads to remote code execution (RCE). Consider refactoring your logic to avoid dynamic evaluation.
Avoid using assert() — behaves like eval() when given a string and can lead to RCE. Use conditional checks directly.
Avoid using create_function() — internally uses eval(), which is dangerous. Use anonymous functions instead.
Avoid using preg_replace() with the /e modifier — it allows execution of arbitrary PHP code and leads to critical security vulnerabilities such as RCE. Use preg_replace_callback() instead.
Avoid using exec() — executes shell commands and leads to command injection vulnerabilities. Use process control functions with strict sanitization only if necessary.
Avoid using shell_exec() — exposes your application to command injection risks. Use safer alternatives or escape all inputs thoroughly.
Avoid using system() — can execute arbitrary system commands. Very dangerous if used with user input.
Avoid using passthru() — runs system-level commands and can be exploited for command injection.
Avoid using popen() — opens a process pipe which can be exploited. Use proc_open() with caution if absolutely necessary.
Avoid using proc_open() — complex function that can lead to RCE or command injection if mishandled.
Avoid using proc_close() — related to proc_open(), which is dangerous if user input is involved.
Avoid using proc_nice() — changes process priority and could be abused.
Avoid using proc_terminate() — interacts with system processes. Use only if trusted input and context are ensured.
Avoid using pcntl_exec() — forks and replaces the process; risky for production environments.
Avoid using fsockopen() with user input — can be used to perform SSRF attacks.
Avoid using pfsockopen() — similar to fsockopen(), risky with user-controlled hosts or ports.
Avoid using phpinfo() in production — exposes sensitive server configuration information.
Avoid using highlight_file() — reveals code, leading to information disclosure.
Avoid using show_source() — exposes raw source code to the browser.
Avoid using fpassthru() — used to dump file contents; dangerous with user-controlled paths.
Avoid using extract() — overwrites existing variables and leads to security issues. Use associative arrays instead.
Avoid using parse_str() — can overwrite variables in the current symbol table. Use parse_url() and parse_str() into a separate array.
Avoid using ini_set() to modify runtime settings unsafely. Changes may weaken security mechanisms.
Avoid using ini_alter() — modifies php.ini settings and can reduce protection if misused.
Avoid using putenv() — changes environment variables at runtime and can affect system behavior.
Avoid using tmpfile() without cleanup — may leak sensitive data to disk.
Avoid using link() — may create unexpected hard links; dangerous with user input.
Avoid using symlink() — can create filesystem links in unsafe locations.
Avoid using virtual() — used in Apache for server-side includes, may execute unintended logic.
Avoid using dl() — enables dynamic loading of extensions at runtime and is dangerous.
Avoid using set_time_limit() — may override execution limits.
Avoid using ignore_user_abort() — continues execution even if client disconnects; risky for long-running scripts.
Avoid using escapeshellcmd() — may offer false sense of security. Better to avoid shell commands entirely.
Avoid using getmyuid() — exposes user identity info, rarely needed in secure apps.
Avoid using leak() — deprecated and dangerous. Leads to memory leaks.
Avoid using listen() — may be part of vulnerable network socket code.
Avoid using diskfreespace() — reveals server storage info.
Avoid using posix_ctermid() — reveals terminal device paths.
Avoid using posix_getcwd() — reveals current directory path.
Avoid using posix_getegid() — exposes process group IDs.
Avoid using posix_geteuid() — exposes user IDs.
Avoid using posix_getgid() — exposes group IDs.
Avoid using posix_getgrgid() — exposes system group info.
Avoid using posix_getgrnam() — exposes system group info.
Avoid using posix_getgroups() — reveals system group memberships.
Avoid using posix_getlogin() — exposes the current login name.
Avoid using posix_getpgid() — exposes process IDs.
Avoid using posix_getpgrp() — exposes process group IDs.
Avoid using posix_getpid() — reveals process IDs.
Avoid using posix_getppid() — reveals parent process IDs.
Avoid using posix_getpwuid() — reveals user info.
Avoid using posix_getrlimit() — reveals system resource limits.
Avoid using posix_getsid() — exposes session IDs.
Avoid using posix_getuid() — exposes user IDs.
Avoid using posix_isatty() — limited use, potentially risky.
Avoid using posix_kill() — sends signals to processes. Dangerous if misused.
Avoid using posix_mkfifo() — creates named pipes; can be abused.
Avoid using posix_setegid() — changes effective group ID; risky.
Avoid using posix_seteuid() — changes effective user ID; risky.
Avoid using posix_setgid() — changes group ID; rarely safe.
Avoid using posix_setpgid() — manipulates process groups.
Avoid using posix_setsid() — creates new session; rarely needed.
Avoid using posix_setuid() — changes user ID; major security risk.
Avoid using posix_times() — returns process times; rarely relevant.
Avoid using posix_ttyname() — reveals terminal device names.
Avoid using posix_uname() — exposes system-level details.
Avoid using socket_accept() — dangerous with custom TCP/IP logic.
Avoid using socket_bind() — binds to ports and may expose services.
Avoid using socket_clear_error() — low-level socket call.
Avoid using socket_close() — low-level function, rarely needed.
Avoid using socket_connect() — potentially dangerous if remote host is user-controlled.
Avoid using socket_listen() — opens services that may be exploited.
Avoid using socket_create_listen() — creates open listening ports.
Avoid using socket_read() — risky if improperly validated.
Avoid using socket_create_pair() — rarely needed in web apps.
Avoid using stream_socket_server() — opens custom servers and may be exploited.
